author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody:
This was perfect. I used raspberry jam, but I can't think of a jam that wouldn't work here. The icing on top is great hint of sweetness.

It was so good, I had to give out slices to make sure I didn't eat the whole thing.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Hate to reheat scones in microwave. Better in toaster oven or regular oven to maintain crunchy exterior! Diced apricot and crystallized ginger are the best add ins.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Has anyone made this as a sheet cake?

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody:
Just made this and it was so delicious.
I added mushrooms 🍄 to the recipe to make it a bit more substantial. So yummy and thank you for the amazing recipe!

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: Made half-batch waiting for Lunar Eclipse at 6 am here in Maine. Sadly, the moon dropped below the tree line. But the cookies were delicious! Plenty of orange flavor with Trader Joe's Seville Orange marmalade. I did add a handful of unsalted, roasted pistachios, in chopped,  which added crunch.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Made this the other night and it was much easier to make than I thought it would be. Heat was perfect and didn't overpower the flavor. There were no leftovers. This will go into the regular menu rotation.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Make the crust with Ginger Snaps (Nabisco, really) rather than graham cracker. The flavor rush is wonderful. You will never go back.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody:
Little dry, would simmer less next time. 
Only had butter beans, worked great. 
Could double the red pepper to get more of a kick. 
Used half and half, worked great
Mozzetta sun dried tomatoes 
Good fresh tomatoes might be a nice addition

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Can I use vegetable stock?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: This recipe one me over; I''m now a fan of salmon! The maple sweet with the tangy flavors- so good. I only had baby spinach which didn't work out too well; next time will use chard which should be heartier. Thank you.

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: In Northern Spain, I discovered that many cooks add a little blue cheese to the recipe. It tastes amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added the zest of the lemon and juice from the lemon to the compote.  Amazing pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In answer to Judith's comment on canned lentils, I found them either at Trader Joe's or Sprouts if you have either of those stores in your state.

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: I didn't use egg and added a little arugula at the end and it was delicious

author: Yotam Ottolenghi
commentBody: Do you think it would be safe to add a cup of water instead of checking it every 30 minutes, which doesn’t work when we are at a Christmas Eve service?

author: Kim Severson
commentBody: Way too much work. This is a lot of steps and bother for what you get.  there are way easier recipes for lemon tart on line.  I think the white chocolate is too sweet in it.  I made it for my husbands birthday because he loves lemon meringue pie and we were disappointed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Even with weights and ceramic pan, my crust collapsed like Vaughan did in the video. But I could press it back up gently and it all worked out. Used rum and covered the slightly browned banana with cream. Will make again.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Delicious recipe. I did end up using a 3tbs scoop as my 1/4 cup cookies were way to large for my taste. I also ended up having to use just mint extract instead of peppermint because that was all that was available. Batter spread, so I used a round cookie cutter to help shape them more. I didn’t end up with a shiny top, nor did the wacking of the pan seem to have any effect. I will have to try again and see if I can get the shiny tops.

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: These were amazing. Huge hit with with everyone. Made per recipe, but next time I might add a sprinkling of parm at the end and use my searzall to add some char. Whole Foods had the Mina harissa.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: I found these cookies to be very disappointing.  I was looking for an orange forward flavor, but there was none. In fact, the only hint of the marmalade was a bitter aftertaste.  I used Bonne Maman which doesn't have a lot of peel.  Perhaps this was the issue.  I wish I had added some orange zest, but I wanted to make as directed.  The other test tasters in my house agreed that this recipe isn't a repeat.

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: This is my go to for scrambled eggs since the first time I tried the recipe  It takes something so ordinary and so easily makes it go to 11.  Fluffy, creamy and delicious.  It's got me wondering what it might do for Quiche.  We will find out later today!

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: Flathead cabbage is fantastic!! It costs more than regular cabbage but is still inexpensive. It’s far better than regular cabbage!

author: David Tanis
commentBody: Do you mean 1 single Tbsp butter for the cake???

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: So so yummy!  Made w/o RR and swapped cream cheese for sour cream to avoid the guar gum!  Resulted in more of a glaze than trad. icing.  Still delish!  Perfect Easter cake!

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Thanks! I’ve wanted a guideline to make such breakfast foods. I don’t add any sweeteners beyond fruit, so i’ll remove honey and add in some soaked dried fruit

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Made as written but halved the quantity and loved it! Crunchiest tofu I’ve ever achieved. Better than most “tofu chorizo” episodes out there. Wish I had made the full recipe so I had more leftovers!

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: I used Bob’s Red Mill 1:1 Gluten Free flour and added 1 tablespoon of orange liquor, and these were delicious

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: Made these tonight with 4 T of "Just Egg" instead of an egg. Next time would use just 3 T; I think the texture was a little too runny. I accidentally added an extra 1/4 t of salt so they were definitely salty, but in the finished version, kind of addictive. I ended up cooking mine for just 7 minutes-- any longer and they were too dark.

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody: Isn’t this exactly Mark Bittman’s recipe from his book How to Cook Everything?

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: This was an excellent dish. I'm looking forward to making it again and trying something other than cream (trying to decrease my cholesterol). Thank you to those who have offered suggestions for substitutions.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: This was really good. I used 2lbs of boneless skinless chicken thighs, which were cooked in 30mins (15 mins pre-asparagus + 15mins with asparagus). I also doubled the paprika and used a lot less oil for the lemon dressing (about 1tbsp). Served it with white sweet potatoes. Turned out great!

author: Emily Weinstein
commentBody:
Made this two years in a row (Thanksgiving )
As I was ever hopeful of these perfect ingredients doing each their best.  Nope. Cheese breaks. Potatoes do not cook through even with so much added time. Greasy mess. Not the luscious cheesy potato dish of your dreams. Do not fall for it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would this recipe work on a grill?

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: I was super excited to make this recipe and had been drooling over the photo for a few weeks! I made it for New Years Eve dinner with family and although it looked beautiful, it was surprisingly not as flavorful as expected. Followed it to a T but very disappointing and will not make again.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
So good!! Some addl tips:
1) burn the skin so it’s crispy
2) add sriracha to the basting
3) make extra jam+mustard for dipping
4) eat with boiled cabbage or sauerkraut to cut the fat&saltiness

author: Claire Saffitz
commentBody: This was delicious exactly as is! I followed instructions completely,  minus the chives because I didn't have them.  When it first came out of the oven, my cake did not appear to hold together well,  but after sitting out to cool it solidified perfectly. It didn't look very pretty to me, but all of the guests at my Thanksgiving meal asked about it and there was very little leftover. I would make this again for sure!

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Fantastic! Easy to assemble and a quick weeknight dish.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: I banged this one out last night and was hailed as a champion of the people! Use a mortar and pestle for the peppercorns. Warm the serving platter if possible. Always keep a bottle of brandy around the house.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: I made this last night...it was very "pesto" flavored and rich...though I wish I thought to cut the recipe in half...(we are just 2 and we will be eating it for days!) It was a little "one note" for me, so perhaps next time I might add some finely chopped salted lemon and some kind of heat...But otherwise lovely!

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Just made this for dinner. So easy, so tasty.

author: Kim Severson
commentBody: As a vegan with celiac's, I'm always looking for interesting vegan GF dishes. I usually have to adapt. Here, I substituted GF panko, but that was the only change. Delicious.

author: Claire Saffitz
commentBody: I'm in Canada and had a lot of trouble sourcing the ingredients for these. For fellow Ontarians: you can get freeze-dried strawberries at Dollarama and the nuts at Bulk Barn. TBD on almond paste – I'm pretty sure I was sold marzipan. We'll see how these turn out...

author: Kay Chun
commentBody: Could you swap the cabbage for eg bok choy?

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: Made these exactly as written. All I can say is that there are better brownie recipes. Don’t bother.

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody:
These cookies were delicious, but mine didn't spread and had a crumbly texture.  I'm measured my ingredients very accurately so I don't think that's the problem. I used a Cuisinart to chop the nuts, which came out quite finely. Do you think that's why the batter was stiff? Also, could it have something to do with the milk powder? Never used that before.
Thanks!

author: Yotam Ottolenghi
commentBody: Help readers - I loved the taste of this, but when I carefully whisked in the miso/brine/lemon combination to the hot oil mix, it seized up and would not incorporate into the oil. The only change I made was to use heavy cream instead of EVOO. (Kidding. I used Italian lemon flavored EVOO) Advice?

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Can I make this with carrots instead?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These look like perfect February pick me up! Has anyone tried this in a 10x10 yet?  It’s either that or a 10” cast iron. Welcome suggested modifications for either. Thank you!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Use Persian cucumber (as the article says), red wine vinegar, and add a tablespoon or two of olive oil.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Really like the inside of these but didn’t like the “overcooked egg” crust. Did anyone else have this problem?

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: The idea of using sweet potato instead is terrific. And it reminds me of an amazing Samin Nosrat NYT recipe involving (steamed!?) sweet potato and tahini butter. So tasty. This is to say, tahini and sweet potato are amazing together. I will try this variation.

author: Kay Chun
commentBody: 10/10... I did it with marinated pork chops (soy sauce/ fish sauce/ garlic) and added furkaki to the rice. TOO good. yuuuum!

author: Tanya Sichynsky
commentBody: I made this for the first time. The pleated parchment paper was better in theory than practice. Thanks to others for the tips offered here. I took the cheesecake to a potluck and the platter looked like it had been scavenged by hyenas.

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: You can also use tauchu or Thai fermented soy beans for the stock.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Ok- let me start this review by stating that all who know me know that I don’t ever cook chicken in my kitchen. Irrational fear of salmonella despite being an otherwise pretty accomplished cook (we all have our issues!). This recipe struck me for some reason. OMG is it sooo good!! Used avocado oil. Served over white rice with lots of lime, avocado, cilantro and a few sliced grape tomatoes (bc the color was nice in my estimation). The heat from the raw red onion and creaminess of sour cream were killer with this fabulous baked combo. Served with a crisp New Zealand  Sav Blanc. I’ve got enough for a few lunches/dinners - assuming I don’t scarf down the rest of it before it hits the fridge. YUM! Thanks NYT Cooking!  Not sure I’m ready to cave on the chicken outside of this recipe- but wow this was worth it.

author: Claire Saffitz
commentBody:
I made this and served it yesterday for an early Thanksgiving. The recipe is really well written and I followed it to a tee, using a metal quarter-sheet pan.

To answer a few questions here:
- You cannot replace the demerara with brown sugar. You could use turbinado though.
- The dessert is not very sweet.
- The dessert is "drier" than pie, but the guests really liked it. I did serve it with unsweetened whipped cream.
- The pastry worked out just great. Do not par bake, not needed.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: These cookies have a lovely texture and the toasted almonds are delicious, but the cookie part needs more flavor. I think I would increase the almond extract and perhaps add imitation butter extract next time. The rum really gets lost in the cooking even with brushing with extra at the end.

author: Claire Saffitz
commentBody:
This recipe is very close to the classic Hershey chocolate cake recipe on the cocoa container with no crème fresh needed. Also rather than use boiling water (as the container says) sub hot coffee.  Therefore, no crème fresh needed, less eggs, and for the comments on how to make without coffee, the original hot water works.
The icing… again use the Hershey container recipe ( my splurge is to use Irish butter).

Everyone asks for the recipe.

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Can I omit espresso powder?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I felt the heavy cream made it so much blander in flavor- just felt like way too much of it.

author: Korsha Wilson
commentBody: I agree with others that this was tasty, but an ordeal to make. Took much longer than 1 1/2 hours. The sauce for the mushrooms was delicious and I hope to make it to augment other recipes.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: It's good! Easy too. Didn't take the casings of the sausage, just cut it in smaller pieces.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: Would rice noodles be okay to make this gluten free?

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: And what is "baharat"?  did I miss an explanation?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I can't obtain limes.  Can I use lemons instead for the epis?

author: Yotam Ottolenghi
commentBody: Unfortunately found it bland and the steamed fish to be an odd texture mixture with the couscous

author: Vaughn Vreeland
commentBody: Definitely some awesome cookies. Have made these twice now sticking as much as possible to the recipe. However, like a number of the comments, I found them to be quite large. And 18 to 20 minutes was not nearly enough cooking time. Ended up cooking them about 25 minutes and they still came out nice and chewy. Next time I think I’ll make them a bit smaller so they’re a little more manageable to eat and then maybe the 18 to 20 minute cooking time will be correct.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: ketchup definitely for that traditional flavor -the cloves and sweetness tilt it toward a non-spicy BBQ  savor.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: i am in awe of melissa clark, but truly, this one is not worth the effort.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Is there a soy free version?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this easy and delicious recipe. I was making it ahead to freeze for company, so stirred the coconut oil and spices in with the rest instead of drizzling it over the top. Will add paneer when reheating to serve. I wanted to mention, I used ground spices instead of whole (coriander, cardamon and cumin), adjusting the quantities accordingly, and the dish still tastes wonderful. You're right, the asafetida adds a wonderful zing.

author: Kay Chun
commentBody: I love braised cabbage. I prefer it cooked to a soft stage with loads of onion and butter. I would think with the brief cook cooking time that this cabbage would still at that ‘squeaky’ stage when you bit into it, if one is using standard green cabbage. For a tenderer result with a quicker cook time, use savoy cabbage or Chinese cabbage. One could complicate things by sautéing the thicker stem pieces with the pork and adding the leafier green sections near the end to add greater textural form.

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody:
I wonder if could the frozen garlic puree from Trader Joe's be used?
1 cube = 1 clove

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned this exact thing from a farmer selling his corn from a stand on the side of a road in PA.  It's amazing.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: Question re: Step 5: Is the goal here for a syrupy texture? So if the wine is sweet, but not syrupy, we still reduce it? Maybe describe what we are looking for first, and then how to get there if it's not quite there.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Sometimes yams (orange, sweeter) are called sweet potatoes, and that’s what I’m thinking is called for here? Classic sweet potatoes are less sweet and light yellow in colour. What’s the consensus here about which type is indicated in the recipe?

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: For those chemistry-curious obsessive this sister article will be of interest… Salt before. Rest for 15mins. Read the whole article to get the deets! Thanks Kenji! https://www.nytimes.com/2024/04/24/dining/best-way-to-salt-scrambled-eggs.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share&referringSource=articleShare&sgrp=c-cb

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody:
I'm new to Asian cooking. Please clarify when I use "toasted" sesame oil versus not toasted. Am I correct that often recipes state only "sesame oil" when meaning toasted?
What is required in this recipe? Thank you!

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Really delicious! I kept the salad separate from the beans. While the salad was tasty too, and the sharp lemon and arugula flavours cut through the rich heaviness of the beans, I found it a bit fussy - next time I might just do a simpler mix of lamb's lettuce and arugula without all the panko etc.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody:
Really was inspired by this meal. It lent itself to all sorts of modifications and was still scrumptious...even for a weeknight. I clocked in at a little over an hour. Here goes:

1/2 & 1/2 instead of heavy cream
Dried thyme
Lacinato kale
Trader Joe’s Harvest Grains Blend (couscous, orzo, quinoa, baby garbanzo beans)
Fresh Pacific cod with blackened spice on both sides (Nice sous vide candidate, but it was after all a weeknight. Still, fresh, it held up well. Don’t overcook!)

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: Try putting a cooking lid on the bowl. It helped me melt the cheese

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: I made it as written, including the rice cakes.  Fabulous.  The heat was spot on for a family dinner.

author: Sam Sifton
commentBody: As a Sichuan native, I approve of this recipe! For me this is a fresh take (sheet panning) on familiar ingredients. Like many others, I used a Lao Gan Ma chili sauce: chili oil with black bean. But really any flavor of LGM should work. Success on my first try. Highly recommended.

author: Yotam Ottolenghi
commentBody: made this to the book — but used anise seeds instead of fennel and my grandmother’s garden basil instead of tarragon (because the tarragon at whole foods was hideously rotted). absolutely hit with everyone from my 87 year old grandmother to my 1 year old baby cousin (having a teeny tiny portion of course) to the family dogs who licked it off the baby’s fingers.

author: Ali Slagle
commentBody: Did anyone else find that when incorporating the sauce into the pasta, it coagulated into a pasty, clumpy mess? We didn’t transfer the pasta to a different dish after boiling it, so we’re thinking the heat of the pot maybe caused this to happen? The sauce looked delicious before we began stirring it in!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I buy precooked bacon bits at Costco.  Most of the fat is already cooked out of it.  I just put a power towel under it in a bowl and zap it for 30 seconds.   It's good in omelettes and other dishes that require bacon.   It's not a whole strip, but almost as good.  A bag Lasts months in the fridge and doesn't need to be refrigerated until opened.

author: Yotam Ottolenghi
commentBody: Delicious! I only had Japanese sweet potatoes on hand, so I used those and it was incredible. Followed the rest of the recipe exactly.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: This was a really nice meal! I used lean ground chicken, so the 5T of oil was absolutely necessary. I doubled the shallots, carrots, and celery, and added an extra garlic clove. I also added slightly less cavatelli, and used sour cream at the end rather than creme fraiche - was really nice with the dill and lemon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: At the risk of offending all the appreciative commentators here, I say no (with good humor)!  I've had lots of nonsense on a hotdog, especially here in Chicago where pickles and tomatoes are bafflingly common.  Again, no! Onion, mustard, and relish only!

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: @Emily — I think the point to melting the butter on the stovetop is that you want to brown the butter. Which, in my opinion, is worth the very little trouble. Overall, though, I found this cornbread quite heavy. If I make it again, I will halve the amount of cheese, just sprinkling it on top a few minutes before taking it out of the oven.

author: Kay Chun
commentBody: I didn't have a sweet potato, but I did have butternut squash, which was a very good substitute.  Easy to make, and everyone liked it--will do again

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: If you have teeth issues, probably better to make it as fried rice. Flavor was very good. Will make it in a wok as fried rice.

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody: Can anyone come up with a good dairy-free version of this amazing-looking dish?

author: Sohla El-Waylly
commentBody: I bet they would be even better with walnuts! (Also, because I don’t have any macadamias.)

author: J. Kenji López-Alt
commentBody: Perfection. I'm from Chicago.

author: Melissa Clark
commentBody: I purchased a fresh turkey labeled10 lb plus a few ounces I forgot to note, and proceeded as instructed to dry brine it over 2 days. I just took it out of the fridge and weighed it, and it is 8lb 14oz. I think the initial weight included the giblets and neck, but could some of the difference be water evaporation? Do I cook according to the weight it is now or the weight I started with?

author: Kay Chun
commentBody: We couldn't find clam juice at our grocery so we subbed chopped clams in their juices (6oz) which worked out well since we were also short on shrimp. We also didn't rinse the beans. Came out so so good, definitely added to our rotation!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@dhwsmith 
I wish the same! I too have tried and failed to find one!  I used to commute on Metro North in the late '80s and early '90s and there was nothing better than that huge bran muffin at the Zaro's kiosk. Alas, I can rarely find a bran muffin anymore at GCT and a NYC deli bran muffin (ahh toasted!) is a distant memory as well...

author: Alexa Weibel
commentBody:
A hit at home with two kids - will be making for a second time this week! 
I used rotisserie chicken instead and it was amazing and quick. The lemon and creme fraiche added flavor and depth.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Im so bummed. I left them in the fridge while we ate dinner and when I took them out to cut, the frosting had separated from the cookie.  So sad.  Still tastes good, but so ugly.
